name: Li Jing General
description: Li Jing, also known as Pagoda-Bearing Heavenly King Li, is a prominent figure in Chinese mythology and a powerful general in the heavenly court. He is often depicted as a stern and imposing figure, clad in armor and carrying a pagoda, which is his most iconic attribute. Li Jing is the father of Nezha, another well-known deity, and their complex relationship is a recurring theme in many stories. Initially, their relationship is strained due to Nezha's mischievous nature and accidental killing of Ao Bing, the Dragon King's son. However, they eventually reconcile, working together to fight evil. Li Jing is revered for his military prowess, strategic thinking, and unwavering loyalty to the Jade Emperor. He commands vast armies and is responsible for maintaining order in the heavens and on Earth. Despite his strict demeanor, Li Jing is ultimately a protector of humanity and a symbol of justice and righteousness. He is often invoked for protection against evil spirits and to bring peace and prosperity. His pagoda possesses immense power, capable of trapping and subduing demons and other malevolent entities. Li Jing's influence extends beyond military matters; he is also associated with fatherhood, authority, and the balance between discipline and compassion. He is a complex and multifaceted character, embodying both the strength of a warrior and the wisdom of a leader.
subtitle: Pagoda-Bearing Heavenly King Li

keyMyths.title: Nezha's Birth and Trouble
keyMyths.description: Li Jing's son, Nezha, is born after three years and six months in his mother's womb. Nezha immediately causes trouble, leading to conflict with the Dragon King Ao Guang.

keyMyths.title: Nezha's Self-Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: To appease the Dragon King and protect his family and the town, Nezha sacrifices himself. He later returns, reborn with the help of a lotus flower.

keyMyths.title: Li Jing's Pagoda
keyMyths.description: The pagoda Li Jing carries is a powerful artifact capable of trapping and subduing demons. It's given to him by a Taoist immortal, enabling him to control Nezha.

sources.author: Xu Zhonglin
sources.description: A major source for the mythology of Li Jing and his family.
sources.text: A major source for the mythology of Li Jing and his family.

sources.author: Wu Cheng'en

associations.name: Pagoda
associations.significance: Represents control, authority, and the ability to imprison demons and spirits.

associations.name: Armor
associations.significance: Symbolizes his role as a protector and warrior.

associations.name: Nezha (Son)
associations.significance: Highlights a complex father-son dynamic, often involving conflict and reconciliation.

associations.name: Heavenly King
associations.significance: Indicates his high rank and authority within the celestial hierarchy.

associations.name: Discipline
associations.significance: Reflects his stern and unwavering adherence to celestial laws and order.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Associated with power, strength, and imperial authority, often depicted on his armor or weapons.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A powerful authority figure within a pantheon, wielding significant power and influence.
A wise and authoritative leader, associated with strategy and command, similar to Li Jing's military role.
God of war, embodies martial prowess and strategic command, paralleling Li Jing's role as a celestial general.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of incense, food, and paper money are made at temples dedicated to Li Jing, seeking his protection and blessings, especially for military endeavors or family harmony.

cultural.modernLegacy: Li Jing remains a popular figure in Chinese opera, television dramas, and video games, often depicted as a wise and powerful general. His image is used to invoke protection and success, especially in business and military contexts.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Ascension
extendedContent.content: While not always explicitly detailed in every version of his myth, Li Jing's origins often depict him as a mortal who attained immortality through rigorous cultivation and virtuous deeds. Some accounts suggest he was a skilled warrior or strategist even before his celestial ascension, demonstrating exceptional leadership and martial capabilities. His devotion to Taoist principles and his unwavering commitment to justice earned him recognition from the celestial court, leading to his appointment as a general. This elevation signifies not only his personal achievements but also the importance of meritocracy and moral conduct in Chinese mythology. His early life, though shrouded in some mystery, establishes the foundation for his later role as a respected and formidable figure within the heavenly hierarchy, setting the stage for his interactions with other deities and his involvement in significant celestial events. This transformation from mortal to immortal general underscores the potential for individuals to achieve greatness through dedication and righteousness.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Li Jing's most renowned exploits involve maintaining order in the mortal realm and suppressing rebellions led by demonic forces. He often commands celestial armies, employing his strategic brilliance and the power of his pagoda to subdue powerful adversaries. His involvement in the subjugation of rebellious spirits and demons highlights his role as a guardian of cosmic balance. A recurring theme in these quests is his complex relationship with his son, Nezha. While their interactions are often fraught with conflict, they ultimately work together to overcome formidable challenges, showcasing the importance of familial bonds amidst adversity. Li Jing's victories are not solely attributed to his martial prowess but also to his wisdom and ability to inspire loyalty among his troops. His leadership ensures the stability of the celestial realm and the protection of humanity from supernatural threats. These battles solidify his reputation as a formidable and respected general, revered for his unwavering dedication to justice and order.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Cultural Impact
extendedContent.content: Li Jing's legacy extends far beyond his military achievements. He embodies the virtues of discipline, strategic thinking, and unwavering commitment to duty. As the Pagoda-Bearing Heavenly King, he symbolizes authority and the power to overcome evil. His image is frequently invoked for protection and success, particularly in military and business contexts. The complex relationship with his son, Nezha, continues to resonate with audiences, exploring themes of familial conflict, reconciliation, and the challenges of parenthood. Li Jing's enduring popularity is evident in his continuous appearances in Chinese opera, television dramas, and video games, solidifying his status as a cultural icon. His story serves as a reminder of the importance of maintaining order, upholding justice, and striving for excellence. He remains a powerful symbol of leadership, protection, and the enduring strength of Chinese mythology, inspiring generations with his unwavering dedication to his celestial duties and his complex, yet ultimately harmonious, family relationships.

shortDescription: Li Jing, the Pagoda-Bearing Heavenly King, is a powerful Chinese deity and general. Father of Nezha, he represents authority and protection.
